insights: Development department processes expenses with a median time of 1.0 day—10 to 14 times faster than all other departments, whose median processing times range from 8.0 to 14.3 days, indicating an extreme structural outlier rather than incremental variation.

Only two users (Cyril Behen and Ramon Amaral) submit expenses to the Development department, and no user in the entire dataset submits to more than one department—making it impossible to disentangle user-specific practices from department-level policies or systems.

Category distribution, expense amounts, CI types, and short_description patterns in Development are statistically indistinguishable from slower departments, ruling out operational scope, financial scale, or asset-class factors as drivers of its speed advantage.

The absence of cross-departmental user behavior and lack of department-level differentiators in available data mean no evidence-based, transferable practice can be identified from Development’s performance—extending its speed to other departments is not supported by current data.

HR department exhibits the slowest processing (median 14.3 days), and the high coefficient of variation (0.438) across departments signals substantial structural imbalance in processing capacity—not uniform inefficiency—suggesting root causes may be department-specific rather than systemic.

Both fast-processing users submit exclusively to Development and show zero overlap with other departments, reinforcing that their speed cannot be assessed independently of Development’s environment—no benchmarking or replication pathway exists in the current data structure.
summary: The goal of identifying transferable practices from Development’s exceptionally fast expense processing (median 1.0 day) cannot be achieved with the available data: a strict 1:1 mapping between users and departments prevents isolating individual behaviors from departmental context, and no department-level operational variables—including category mix, CI types, expense amounts, or description patterns—distinguish Development from slower departments. The extreme inter-department variation (10–14× speed difference) reflects structural segmentation rather than scalable process design. Since no evidence supports generalizable mechanisms—and HR’s severe slowdown (14.3-day median) further underscores department-specific drivers—the priority should shift to investigating internal controls, approval workflows, or system access differences within Development and HR specifically, rather than cross-departmental rollout.
